title: Session 9: Recap and Looking Ahead
text:
Note



Date: Thursday, 18 June 2026, 16:00–19:00

title: Session 9: Recap and Looking Ahead
text:
Learning goals

Consolidate key concepts from the full course arc (description → association → causation → communication)
Reflect on the inference vs. prediction distinction: when do we estimate to understand, and when to forecast?
Discuss what comes next: an introduction to machine learning thinking and where to go from here
Prepare for the final exam: what to expect and how to approach it

title: Session 9: Recap and Looking Ahead
text:
Course arc recap







Sessions
Theme




1–4
Regression toolkit: multiple predictors, binary outcomes, diagnostics


5
Causal thinking: confounding, selection bias, the identification problem


6
Panel data: fixed effects and unobserved heterogeneity


7–8
Modern workflow: AI-assisted coding and data communication


9
Consolidation: inference vs. prediction, looking ahead

title: Session 9: Recap and Looking Ahead
text:
The inference vs. prediction distinction
One of the most important conceptual lines in applied data science: are we estimating a coefficient to understand a relationship, or are we building a model to make forecasts? These require different choices — in model complexity, evaluation criteria, and how we report results. This session addresses the distinction conceptually, without a dedicated technical session on machine learning.






Important



Material will be available here before the session.

title: Session 7: Coding Smarter — R and AI Tools
text:
Note



Date: Thursday, 04 June 2026, 16:00–19:00

title: Session 7: Coding Smarter — R and AI Tools
text:
Business question
Does working from home improve or reduce employee performance — and can we use AI tools to analyse this efficiently?

title: Session 7: Coding Smarter — R and AI Tools
text:
Learning goals

Use AI tools effectively for R coding, debugging, and documentation
Understand the limitations and risks of AI-generated code (hallucinations, silent errors)
Develop a critical and efficient AI-assisted workflow
Verify and test AI output rather than trusting it blindly

title: Session 7: Coding Smarter — R and AI Tools
text:
Dataset
working-from-home — 249 employees from a randomised controlled experiment at a Chinese travel agency (Bloom et al., 2015). Performance (calls per minute), WFH assignment, tenure, demographics.

title: Session 7: Coding Smarter — R and AI Tools
text:
Quarto skill introduced this session
Using AI to write, debug, and improve Quarto documents: prompt strategies, reviewing AI-generated prose, using AI for formatting and cross-referencing.






Important



Material will be available here before the session.

title: Session 5: Causation vs. Correlation — Thinking Like an Economist
text:
Note



Date: Thursday, 21 May 2026, 16:00–19:00

title: Session 5: Causation vs. Correlation — Thinking Like an Economist
text:
Business question
Is there a gender wage gap among market analysts — and if so, what explains it?

title: Session 5: Causation vs. Correlation — Thinking Like an Economist
text:
Learning goals

Distinguish causal from associational claims
Understand confounding and selection bias through concrete business examples
See how adding controls changes an estimated gap — and what that means
Critically evaluate causal claims in reports and media

title: Session 5: Causation vs. Correlation — Thinking Like an Economist
text:
Dataset
cps-earnings — 149,316 US workers from the 2014 Current Population Survey: hourly wage, gender, age, education, occupation. The gender wage gap is a textbook case for illustrating confounding.

title: Session 5: Causation vs. Correlation — Thinking Like an Economist
text:
Quarto skill introduced this session
Structuring an analytical narrative in Quarto: how to move from exploratory output to a coherent argument in a written report.






Important



Material will be available here before the session.

title: Session 3: Modelling Binary and Categorical Outcomes
text:
Note



Date: Thursday, 30 April 2026, 16:00–19:00

title: Session 3: Modelling Binary and Categorical Outcomes
text:
Business question
Can we predict which firms are likely to exit the market in the next year?

title: Session 3: Modelling Binary and Categorical Outcomes
text:
Learning goals

Understand when and why OLS breaks down for binary outcomes
Run and interpret logistic regression with glm()
Translate log-odds into meaningful business insights (predicted probabilities, marginal effects)

title: Session 3: Modelling Binary and Categorical Outcomes
text:
Dataset
bisnode-firms — 19,036 firms in manufacturing and services: firm exit (binary), size, financial indicators, management variables.

title: Session 3: Modelling Binary and Categorical Outcomes
text:
Quarto skill introduced this session
Inline R code for automatic result reporting: embedding model outputs directly in text so numbers update automatically when the data or model changes.






Important



Material will be available here before the session.

title: Welcome, Recap & Tooling Upgrade
text:
What is this course?
Building on what you know: R, Quarto, tidyverse, basic linear regression — all from Research Methodology (Fall 2025)
What we add:

Regression done properly: multiple predictors, binary outcomes, diagnostics
Causal thinking: why correlation ≠ causation, and what to do about it
Panel data, modern tools, professional communication

The rule: Every session starts with a business question, not a method name

Not: “Today we learn logistic regression” But: “Which firms are likely to exit the market — and can we predict it?”

title: Welcome, Recap & Tooling Upgrade
text:
Quarto in this course
Why Quarto matters:

Code and text in one place — no copy-pasting numbers into Word
Results update automatically when data or code changes
Renders to HTML, PDF, Word — same source file

The standard we set today applies to every submission:

Proper YAML front matter
Numbered sections, table of contents
Bibliography with BibTeX citations
Clean, commented code chunks


Starting from Task 1, every submission is a rendered Quarto HTML file. Today you build that habit.

title: Welcome, Recap & Tooling Upgrade
section: Git: the four things you need
text:
Git: the four things you need





flowchart LR
  A["📁<br/>Working directory"] -->|"Stage (Git pane)"| B["📋<br/>Staging area"]
  B -->|"Commit (+ message)"| C["💾<br/>Local repository"]
  C -->|"Push"| D["☁️<br/>GitHub (remote)"]
  D -->|"Pull"| A

  style A fill:#f4f6f8,stroke:#00395B
  style B fill:#f4f6f8,stroke:#69AACD
  style C fill:#f4f6f8,stroke:#00395B
  style D fill:#00395B,color:#ffffff,stroke:#00395B






You only need these four actions. No branching, no merging.
All of this happens in the RStudio Git pane — no command line.

For a full introduction: GitHub Skills — Introduction to GitHub

title: Welcome, Recap & Tooling Upgrade
text:
Take-home task 1
You are an analyst producing a briefing on global development patterns.
Dataset: World Bank — life expectancy and GDP per capita, 182 countries, 2017 Download from OSF: osf.io/pguwf
Five tasks:

Load and explore — summary statistics, two plots
Simple regression: life expectancy ~ GDP per capita — interpret the coefficient
Log-transform GDP — does fit improve? Why?
Add one variable, compare models with a modelsummary table
Write a 150-word plain-language summary for a non-technical reader

Deadline: 02 April 2026, 08:00 Submit: GitHub Classroom (link below) | Moodle fallback
GitHub Classroom link — to be added

Post questions in GitHub Discussions — I will check regularly








Tip


If something in Task 1 feels unfamiliar, that is a signal — not a problem. The two weeks are designed for exactly this.

PDF export (Chrome/Edge): click ☰ bottom-left → Tools → PDF Export Mode → print. Alternative: add ?print-pdf to the URL, then print.

title: Session 1 — Exercise
text:
Scenario: You are a junior analyst at an international consultancy. Before writing any analysis, your team requires all reports to follow a standard document structure — a proper YAML header, numbered sections, and a bibliography. Your task today is to build that structure from scratch.
You are not analysing data yet. You are building the container that will hold your analysis for the rest of the semester.
Work through the six tasks below. Each task has a check — a short description of what you should see when you have done it correctly.

title: Session 1 — Exercise
text:
Scenario: You are a junior analyst at an international consultancy. Before writing any analysis, your team requires all reports to follow a standard document structure — a proper YAML header, numbered sections, and a bibliography. Your task today is to build that structure from scratch.
You are not analysing data yet. You are building the container that will hold your analysis for the rest of the semester.
Work through the six tasks below. Each task has a check — a short description of what you should see when you have done it correctly.

title: Session 1 — Exercise
text:
2 Task 1: Project setup
Set up a clean R project with the standard folder structure.
Steps:

In RStudio, go to File → New Project → New Directory → New Project
Give it a meaningful name (e.g. my-analysis) and choose a sensible location
Once the project opens, create three folders inside it:

data/ — for raw data files
figures/ — for exported plots
output/ — for rendered reports

Create a new Quarto document: File → New File → Quarto Document Save it as report.qmd in your project root







Tip



How to create folders: In the RStudio Files pane (bottom right), click New Folder.


Check: Your Files pane should show:
my-analysis/
├── my-analysis.Rproj
├── report.qmd
├── data/
├── figures/
└── output/

title: Session 1 — Exercise
text:
3 Task 2: YAML front matter
Replace the default YAML header in report.qmd with a complete one.
Steps:
Delete whatever Quarto put in the YAML by default and replace it with this — filling in your own title and name:
---
title: "Your Report Title"
author: "Your Name"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
  docx:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
bibliography: references.bib
---
Check: The document has a YAML block between --- markers at the top. If you try to render now, it will warn about a missing references.bib — that is expected and fixed in Task 4.






Note



date: today tells Quarto to use today’s date automatically every time the document renders — no manual updating needed.

title: Session 1 — Exercise
text:
4 Task 3: Document structure
Add meaningful content sections to your report.
Steps:
Below the YAML, write at least three sections using Markdown headings. Choose a topic relevant to international business — it does not need to be sophisticated. For example:
## Introduction

This report examines ... [one or two sentences].

## Data

The analysis uses data from ... [one or two sentences].

## Results

The main finding is ... [one or two sentences].
Check: When you render to HTML (Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + K), you should see:

A numbered table of contents on the left
Three numbered sections in the document body

title: Session 1 — Exercise
text:
5 Task 4: Bibliography
Add citations to your report using BibTeX.
Steps:

Copy the file references.bib from the session folder into your project folder (next to report.qmd)
In your document text, add at least two citations using @key syntax. The keys available in the provided file are:

@BekezKezdi2021 — the main course textbook
@Wooldridge2020 — an econometrics reference
@Preston1975 — a classic applied paper

At the end of your document, add a ## References heading (Quarto fills this in automatically)
Render to HTML and confirm the reference list appears

Example citation in text:
As discussed in @BekezKezdi2021, regression analysis requires ...

For a formal treatment, see @Wooldridge2020.

## References
Check: The rendered HTML shows in-text citations (e.g. “Békés & Kézdi, 2021”) and a formatted reference list at the end.






Note



Zotero integration (optional): Zotero can generate .bib entries automatically and sync with Quarto. This is covered in an optional tutorial after the session — not required today.

title: Session 1 — Exercise
text:
6 Task 5: R code chunk
Add a working R code chunk that produces a plot.
Steps:

Insert a code chunk: Ctrl/Cmd + Alt + I (or use the Insert menu)
In the chunk, load the tidyverse and make any simple plot using built-in data

A minimal example to get started:
```{r}
#| label: fig-example
#| fig-cap: "A simple example plot."

library(tidyverse)

ggplot(mpg, aes(x = displ, y = hwy, colour = class)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(
    x = "Engine displacement (litres)",
    y = "Highway fuel economy (mpg)"
  )
```
You can use any built-in dataset and any ggplot2 plot you like — this is just to confirm the workflow runs end to end.
Check: When you render, a figure appears in the HTML output with a caption.

title: Session 1 — Exercise
text:
7 Task 6: Render to both formats
Confirm your document renders cleanly to both HTML and Word.
Steps:

Render to HTML: Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + K (or click the Render button) The output appears in the Viewer pane or opens in your browser
Render to Word: in the Render button dropdown, select docx (or add format: docx temporarily and re-render)

Check: Both files exist in your project:

report.html — opens in a browser, shows TOC, numbered sections, figure, references
report.docx — opens in Word with the same structure







Tip



The .docx output may look slightly different from the HTML — that is normal. Word rendering is polished in Session 8.

title: Session 1 — Exercise
text:
8 Finished early?

Try adding a second code chunk with a different plot or a summary table
Explore YAML options: what does code-fold: true do? What about theme: cosmo?
Read the Quarto documentation on HTML documents

title: Getting Started
text:
This course uses three tools throughout:

R — statistical computing language (you already know this from the prior course)
Quarto — reproducible document and report system
GitHub — version control and collaboration platform

title: Getting Started
text:
This course uses three tools throughout:

R — statistical computing language (you already know this from the prior course)
Quarto — reproducible document and report system
GitHub — version control and collaboration platform

title: Getting Started
text:
GitHub account
You need a GitHub account to access course materials and submit take-home tasks.
If you do not have one yet, create a free account at github.com. Once you have an account, we strongly recommend registering for the GitHub Student Developer Pack, which gives you access to a range of professional tools for free:
The Student Pack also unlocks GitHub Codespaces — a browser-based RStudio environment that requires zero local installation. This is the recommended way to work in this course.

title: Getting Started
text:
Coding environment
Option A — GitHub Codespaces (recommended): No installation needed. All required R packages come pre-installed.

Go to the course material repository on GitHub
Click the green Code button → Codespaces → Create codespace on main
RStudio opens in your browser — you are ready to work

Option B — local RStudio: Install R 4.4+, RStudio, and run:
install.packages(c("tidyverse", "plm", "modelsummary", "fixest",
                   "lmtest", "sandwich", "patchwork"))

title: Getting Started
section: Git in this course: four things you need
text:
Git in this course: four things you need
You interact with Git via the RStudio Git pane — no command line required.



Action
What it does




Pull
Get the latest version from GitHub


Stage
Select which changed files to include in your snapshot


Commit
Save a snapshot with a short description message


Push
Send your snapshot to GitHub



That is all you need. No branching, no merging. For a full introduction, see the GitHub Skills: Introduction to GitHub tutorial.

title: Getting Started
text:
Questions and discussion
All course questions go through GitHub Discussions on the material repository (link shared in Session 1). This keeps the discussion visible to everyone and searchable.
Moodle is used for official announcements and grades only.

title: Getting Started
text:
Material
An overview of all sessions and materials is on the Schedule & Overview page. Session pages are added progressively as the course proceeds.

title: Data Science and Econometrics for International Business
text:
What: A hands-on seminar equipping second-year master’s students with the practical data science and econometric skills needed for applied research in international business studies. The course follows a deliberate arc — from strengthening regression skills, through causal thinking, to modern data communication. Skills in using tools such as Github and Quarto for Communication, which are central in the contemporary environment, will receive extra attention.
How: Every session starts with a business question, not a method name. Concepts are introduced through intuition and real examples, followed by live coding and a 60-minute hands-on exercise with real data.
Prerequisites: Basic knowledge in R, Quarto, and basic linear regression. All requirements are covered in the required course Theoretical and Empirical Research Methodology, the material of which is available here.).

title: Data Science and Econometrics for International Business
text:
What: A hands-on seminar equipping second-year master’s students with the practical data science and econometric skills needed for applied research in international business studies. The course follows a deliberate arc — from strengthening regression skills, through causal thinking, to modern data communication. Skills in using tools such as Github and Quarto for Communication, which are central in the contemporary environment, will receive extra attention.
How: Every session starts with a business question, not a method name. Concepts are introduced through intuition and real examples, followed by live coding and a 60-minute hands-on exercise with real data.
Prerequisites: Basic knowledge in R, Quarto, and basic linear regression. All requirements are covered in the required course Theoretical and Empirical Research Methodology, the material of which is available here.).

title: Data Science and Econometrics for International Business
text:
Key information



Time
Thursdays, 16:00–19:00


Period
12 March – 18 June 2026


In-person sessions
9 (including recap)


Tools
R, RStudio (via GitHub Codespaces if desired), Quarto

title: Data Science and Econometrics for International Business
text:
Learning objectives
By the end of the course, students will be able to:

Use tools such as Github, which are essential in a working environment in which AI becomes increasingly important
Interpret multiple regression output and communicate results to a non-technical audience
Recognise when and why standard OLS breaks down, and apply appropriate fixes
Distinguish causal from associational claims and critically evaluate causal arguments
Apply panel data methods with fixed effects
Use AI tools critically and effectively for R coding and debugging
Produce publication-ready figures and reports in Quarto


 Moodle (link to be added) 
 GitHub Material Repo

title: Seminar Description
text:
This seminar equips second-year master’s students with the practical data science and econometric skills needed for applied research in international business. It builds directly on Theoretical and Empirical Research Methodology (Fall 2025), in which students learned R, Quarto, and basic linear regression.
The course follows a deliberate arc: from strengthening regression skills, through causal thinking, to modern data communication. The emphasis throughout is on intuition, real-world application, and reproducible workflow — not mathematical derivation.

title: Seminar Description
text:
This seminar equips second-year master’s students with the practical data science and econometric skills needed for applied research in international business. It builds directly on Theoretical and Empirical Research Methodology (Fall 2025), in which students learned R, Quarto, and basic linear regression.
The course follows a deliberate arc: from strengthening regression skills, through causal thinking, to modern data communication. The emphasis throughout is on intuition, real-world application, and reproducible workflow — not mathematical derivation.

title: Seminar Description
text:
2 Course Information



2.1 Logistics

Programme: M.A. International Business Studies, Year 2
Institution: Europa-Universität Flensburg
Instructor: Prof. Dr. Claudius Gräbner-Radkowitsch
Time: Thursdays, 16:00–19:00
Period: 12 March – 18 June 2026
In-person sessions: 9 (including recap)




2.2 Tools

Language: R
IDE: RStudio (via GitHub Codespaces)
Reporting: Quarto (HTML and Word output)
Key packages: tidyverse, ggplot2, modelsummary, fixest, lmtest, sandwich, patchwork, plm
Bibliography: BibTeX / Zotero

title: Seminar Description
text:
3 Prior Knowledge
Students have completed Theoretical and Empirical Research Methodology (Fall 2025), which covered:

R programming and RStudio (data wrangling, basic visualisation with ggplot2)
Quarto for reproducible reports
Foundational statistics: probability, sampling theory, hypothesis testing
Simple and basic multiple linear regression (introduction only)

title: Seminar Description
text:
4 Git and GitHub
A key skill developed in this course is working with Git and GitHub — the version control and collaboration infrastructure that underlies virtually all professional data work, and that is increasingly essential in an environment where AI tools are integrated into everyday coding workflows.
Students are expected to create a free GitHub account before Session 1 and to register for the GitHub Student Developer Pack. Basic Git operations (pull, stage, commit, push) are introduced in Session 1 and used throughout the course. No prior experience is required; the GitHub Skills: Introduction to GitHub tutorial provides a self-contained introduction.

title: Seminar Description
text:
5 Pedagogical Approach
Each 3-hour in-person session follows roughly the following structure:



Time
Activity




60 min
Concept introduction: intuition, visuals, real examples


30 min
Live coding demonstration


60 min
Hands-on student exercise with a real dataset


30 min
Debrief, interpretation, Q&A



Core principles:

Always lead with a business question
Prioritise interpretation over mathematical derivation
Use consistent datasets across multiple sessions where possible
Embed Quarto communication skills progressively

title: Seminar Description
text:
6 Thematic Arc
The course follows a progression from description → association → causation → communication:

Regression toolkit (Sessions 2–4): Deepen regression skills — multiple predictors, binary outcomes, diagnostics
Causal thinking (Session 5): What separates a valid causal claim from a spurious correlation
Panel methods (Session 6): Using longitudinal structure to control for unobservables
Modern workflow (Sessions 7–8): AI-assisted coding and professional data communication
Recap & outlook (Session 9): Consolidation and the inference vs. prediction distinction

title: Seminar Description
text:
7 Assessment

7.1 Final Exam
An open-book, on-site exam (details to be confirmed). Students may bring any materials but work individually on their own laptops.


7.2 Take-Home Tasks (mandatory, pass/fail)
Two structured exercises submitted as rendered Quarto HTML reports during instructor-unavailable periods. Pass criteria: all tasks attempted, code runs without errors, results are interpreted in plain language.








Task
Dataset
Due




Task 1 — Regression basics
worldbank-lifeexpectancy

title: Income and Life Expectancy: A Global Perspective
text:
How does income relate to health? This question sits at the heart of development economics — and it turns out the answer is both intuitive and surprising.
At first glance, richer countries do tend to have longer-living populations. But the relationship is not linear: the difference in life expectancy between a country with a GDP per capita of $2,000 and one with $10,000 is much larger than the difference between $50,000 and $100,000. This pattern was first documented systematically by Preston (1975) and has become one of the most replicated findings in the social sciences.
In this report, we explore this relationship using cross-country data from the World Bank. The central question is: how much of the variation in life expectancy across countries can be explained by income alone?
The data and methods follow Békés and Kézdi (2021), who use this dataset as a running example for visualisation and regression.

title: Income and Life Expectancy: A Global Perspective
text:
How does income relate to health? This question sits at the heart of development economics — and it turns out the answer is both intuitive and surprising.
At first glance, richer countries do tend to have longer-living populations. But the relationship is not linear: the difference in life expectancy between a country with a GDP per capita of $2,000 and one with $10,000 is much larger than the difference between $50,000 and $100,000. This pattern was first documented systematically by Preston (1975) and has become one of the most replicated findings in the social sciences.
In this report, we explore this relationship using cross-country data from the World Bank. The central question is: how much of the variation in life expectancy across countries can be explained by income alone?
The data and methods follow Békés and Kézdi (2021), who use this dataset as a running example for visualisation and regression.

title: Income and Life Expectancy: A Global Perspective
text:
2 Data and approach
We use a cross-section of 182 countries observed in 2017. The two key variables are:

Life expectancy (years): average life expectancy at birth
GDP per capita (USD, PPP-adjusted): a standard measure of average income

The data are publicly available from the World Bank World Development Indicators and can be downloaded from OSF.
For this demonstration, we use a small illustrative dataset to show the code structure. In your own work, replace example_data with the real dataset.

title: Income and Life Expectancy: A Global Perspective
text:
3 Results

3.1 The income–life expectancy relationship


Code
# Illustrative dataset — replace with real World Bank data for your own analysis
example_data <- tibble(
  country        = c("Nigeria", "India", "Brazil", "China",
                     "Mexico", "Poland", "Germany", "USA", "Norway"),
  gdp_per_capita = c(5300, 6600, 14100, 16800,
                     19900, 31900, 53700, 63500, 67300),
  life_expectancy = c(54, 69, 75, 76,
                      75, 78, 81, 78, 82)
)

ggplot(example_data, aes(x = gdp_per_capita, y = life_expectancy)) +
  geom_point(colour = "#00395B", size = 3, alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = country),
    nudge_y = 0.6, size = 3.2, colour = "#6F6F6F"
  ) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::label_dollar(scale = 1/1000, suffix = "k")) +
  labs(
    x     = "GDP per capita (log scale, USD PPP)",
    y     = "Life expectancy (years)",
    title = "Richer countries tend to live longer",
    subtitle = "But the relationship flattens at high income levels"
  )








Figure 1: Life expectancy and GDP per capita across selected countries (illustrative data). A log scale on the x-axis straightens the curved relationship.





Figure 1 shows a clear positive association: countries with higher incomes tend to have longer life expectancies. Notice that the x-axis uses a log scale — this straightens the curved relationship and makes the pattern easier to read. We will return to this choice in Session 2.


3.2 A first regression


Code
library(modelsummary)   # for clean regression tables

# Add the log-transformed predictor
example_data <- example_data |>
  mutate(log_gdp = log(gdp_per_capita))

# Fit the model
model <- lm(life_expectancy ~ log_gdp, data = example_data)

# Display results
modelsummary(
  model,
  stars   = TRUE,
  gof_map = c("nobs", "r.squared")
)




Table 1: OLS regression of life expectancy on log GDP per capita.





    

    
    
      
        
        
              
                 
                (1)
              
        
        + p < 0.1, * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001
        
                
                  (Intercept)
                  -2.662
                
                
                  
                  (17.935)
                
                
                  log_gdp
                  7.689**
                
                
                  
                  (1.787)
                
                
                  Num.Obs.
                  9
                
                
                  R2
                  0.726
                
        
      
    






The coefficient on log GDP per capita tells us: for each 1% increase in GDP per capita, life expectancy increases by approximately 0.08 years on average.

title: Income and Life Expectancy: A Global Perspective
text:
4 Conclusion
Even a simple regression reveals a strong positive relationship between income and life expectancy. However, income alone cannot explain all of the variation — some countries outperform their income level substantially, while others fall below it. Unpacking these patterns requires controlling for additional variables, a topic we return to in Sessions 2 and 4.

title: Session 1: Welcome, Recap & Tooling Upgrade
text:
Learning goals

Understand course structure, expectations, and assessment
Refresh key R and Quarto skills from the prior course
Set up a clean, reproducible project workflow (.Rproj, relative paths, folder structure)
Upgrade Quarto skills: YAML front matter, multiple output formats, bibliography with BibTeX
Understand the basics of Git version control via the RStudio Git pane







Important



All questions should be posted on the dedicated discussion page. This allows all students to benefit from responses and allows you to help each other. I do not answer questions via email.

title: Session 1: Welcome, Recap & Tooling Upgrade
text:
Materials



File
Description




Slides
Lecture slides — open in browser, press F for fullscreen


Demo document
Live coding document built during the session; use as a model for your own reports


Exercise sheet
In-session exercise — setting up a reproducible Quarto workflow


References
BibTeX file with all citations used in session materials









TipDownloading the source (.qmd) files





The demo document and exercise sheet have a </> Code button in the top-right corner. Click it and select “View Source” to see the raw .qmd file. Copy the full text, paste it into a new file in RStudio, and save it with a .qmd extension — you now have a fully working local copy.









TipSaving slides as PDF





Option A (recommended): Open the slides in Chrome or Edge, click ☰ (bottom-left) → Tools → PDF Export Mode, then File → Print → Save as PDF.
Option B: Add ?print-pdf to the end of the URL, then File → Print → Save as PDF. Enable “Background graphics” in the print options for correct styling.

title: Session 2: Multiple Regression — Going Beyond the Basics
text:
Note



Date: Thursday, 09 April 2026, 16:00–19:00

title: Session 2: Multiple Regression — Going Beyond the Basics
text:
Business question
What features drive hotel prices in Vienna, once we control for location?

title: Session 2: Multiple Regression — Going Beyond the Basics
text:
Learning goals

Interpret multiple regression output with confidence
Understand the role of control variables and what “holding constant” means
Compare model fit and interpret standardised coefficients
Communicate regression results to a non-technical audience

title: Session 2: Multiple Regression — Going Beyond the Basics
text:
Dataset
hotels-vienna — 428 hotels in Vienna: price, distance from city centre, star rating, review scores.

title: Session 2: Multiple Regression — Going Beyond the Basics
text:
Quarto skill introduced this session
Regression tables with modelsummary: formatting, captions, model comparison.

title: Session 4: What Can Go Wrong — Biases and Diagnostics
text:
Note



Date: Thursday, 07 May 2026, 16:00–19:00

title: Session 4: What Can Go Wrong — Biases and Diagnostics
text:
Business question
Does the hotel price–distance relationship hold across all European cities, or does it break down in some?

title: Session 4: What Can Go Wrong — Biases and Diagnostics
text:
Learning goals

Identify key OLS assumption violations in practice
Detect multicollinearity, heteroskedasticity, and influential outliers
Understand omitted variable bias intuitively
Apply robust standard errors using lmtest and sandwich

title: Session 4: What Can Go Wrong — Biases and Diagnostics
text:
Dataset
hotels-europe — 148,021 hotel-date observations across 46 European cities and 10 dates. The larger and more heterogeneous sample makes OLS assumption violations more visible.

title: Session 4: What Can Go Wrong — Biases and Diagnostics
text:
Quarto skill introduced this session
Embedding diagnostic plots in a report: figure captions, cross-references (@fig-), and layout options for multi-panel figures.






Important



Material will be available here before the session.

title: Session 6: Panel Data and Fixed Effects
text:
Note



Date: Thursday, 28 May 2026, 16:00–19:00

title: Session 6: Panel Data and Fixed Effects
text:
Business question
Does better management quality lead to better firm performance — and does this relationship hold within countries?

title: Session 6: Panel Data and Fixed Effects
text:
Learning goals

Understand the logic of panel data (units observed over time)
Apply fixed effects estimation to control for unobserved unit-level heterogeneity
Distinguish within- vs. between-variation and interpret each
Use fixest for efficient fixed effects estimation

title: Session 6: Panel Data and Fixed Effects
text:
Dataset
wms-management-survey — manufacturing firms across 24 countries, 2004–2015 (World Management Survey). Management quality scores, firm characteristics, performance indicators.

title: Session 6: Panel Data and Fixed Effects
text:
Quarto skill introduced this session
Presenting panel model output cleanly with fixest and modelsummary: handling multiple fixed effects, notes on standard error clustering.






Important



Material will be available here before the session.

title: Session 8: Communicating Data — Advanced Visualization
text:
Business question
How do health outcomes relate to income globally — and how do we show this clearly?

title: Session 8: Communicating Data — Advanced Visualization
text:
Learning goals

Create publication-ready figures with ggplot2 and patchwork
Visualise uncertainty and model results effectively
Label outliers, annotate trends, and use colour intentionally
Adapt output for different audiences and formats (HTML vs. Word)

title: Session 8: Communicating Data — Advanced Visualization
text:
Datasets

worldbank-lifeexpectancy — 182 countries: life expectancy and GDP per capita, 2017
world-bank-immunization — 172 countries, 1998–2017: immunisation rates and child survival

Together these datasets enable scatter plots, line plots over time, faceted comparisons, and annotated figures — the full range of ggplot2 capabilities.

title: Session 8: Communicating Data — Advanced Visualization
text:

Publication-ready figures: figure sizing, cross-references, captions, and exporting to Word with correct figure placement.
